1: el chikkekha shofar ka-nesher al beit YHWH: 'Set the trumpet to your lips! An enemy swoops like an eagle against the house of the LORD.' The shofar (trumpet) blast announces imminent invasion. The eagle (nesher) swooping against the house of YHWH is the covenant curse of Deut 28:49: 'The LORD will bring a nation against you from far away, from the end of the earth, like an eagle swooping down.' What follows in Deut 28:50-68 is the detailed covenant curse that Hosea applies to the Assyrian invasion. 'They have transgressed my covenant and rebelled against my law' (torati) — the two covenant instruments (covenant and Torah) are named as the basis for the judgment announced.
2: li yiz'aqu Elohai yedanucha Yisrael: 'Israel cries out to me, My God, we know you — we are Israel!' The community claims covenant knowledge — yedanucha (we know you) — in the moment of crisis, but Hosea's entire context shows this knowledge is superficial. Da'at Elohim (knowledge of God) is the covenant relationship's interior content, not merely recognition; 4:1 says 'there is no faithfulness, no steadfast love, and no knowledge of God in the land.' Claiming covenant identity ('we are Israel') while lacking its substance is the religious crisis Hosea diagnoses. The appeal to YHWH in crisis without the corresponding covenant life is the contradiction the book exposes.
3: zenavh Yisrael tov oyev yirdefenu: 'Israel has thrown away what is good; an enemy will pursue him.' Zenavh (throw away, reject with disgust) + tov (what is good) — the good that Israel rejects is the covenant relationship, Torah, and YHWH's way of life. The consequence is automatic: rejection of the good produces pursuit by the enemy. This is the covenant logic of Leviticus 26 and Deuteronomy 28: rejection of YHWH results in pursuit by enemies. The brevity of v3 gives it aphoristic weight — a one-line summary of covenant theology: reject the good, be pursued by evil.
4: hem himliku velo mimeni hisiru velo yadata: 'They have installed kings, but not by my authority; they have set up princes without my approval.' The northern kingdom's political history was marked by self-appointed kings (the sequence of usurpers from Jeroboam II onward). YHWH's authority over kingship — established in Deuteronomy 17 and exercised through prophetic anointing (1 Sam 9; 1 Kgs 1) — has been bypassed. With their silver and gold they made idols (atzabbim), 'so that he might be cut off' (lema'an yikaret) — the idol manufacture is the means of their own covenant cutting-off. The word for 'cut off' (karat) is the covenant-making term's dark inverse: the very relationship that was karat (cut, established) in covenant is now karat (cut off) by apostasy.
5: zanach eglekh Shomron charah api bam: 'Your calf-idol is rejected, O Samaria; my anger burns against them.' The golden calf at Bethel/Samaria was erected by Jeroboam I (1 Kgs 12:28-29) as a religious alternative to Jerusalem, with the words: 'Here are your gods, O Israel, who brought you up out of Egypt.' Hosea's refutation is exegetical: 'How long before they are capable of innocence?' (ad matai lo yukhal nezakion) — innocence (naqah, purity, being cleared of guilt) is the covenantal goal that the idol-worshipping northern kingdom cannot achieve. The question implies that purity is possible but perpetually deferred by their apostasy.
6: ki miYisrael vehu charash asahu vehu lo Elohim: 'For it comes from Israel! A craftsman made it; it is not God.' The theological argument against the calf-idol is anthropological: it was made by a craftsman (charash), it comes from Israel's own hands, therefore it cannot be God. This is Hosea's version of the idol-polemic developed more fully in Isaiah 44:9-20 and Psalm 115:4-8. 'The calf of Samaria will be shattered into pieces' (shivvim yihyeh eghel Shomron) — shivvim (splinters, chips) is the ignominious end of the thing they thought was their god.
7: ki ruach yizre'u vesuphah yiqtzoru: 'For they have sown the wind and shall reap the whirlwind.' One of the OT's great aphoristic formulations. Ruach (wind, breath, spirit) sown produces suphah (whirlwind, hurricane) reaped — the principle of proportional, intensified consequences. Sowing something insubstantial (wind) produces not a harvest of wind but a harvest exponentially more destructive than the sowing. 'The stalk has no bud; it will produce no flour' — the agricultural futility extends the metaphor: their activity (sowing) produces not just nothing but escalating disaster. Paul applies this agricultural consequence principle to the moral life (Gal 6:7-8: 'you reap what you sow').
8: nivla Yisrael attah hayu vagoyim kivli yechetz lahem: 'Israel is swallowed up; they are now among the nations like a worthless piece of pottery.' Nivla (swallowed up) — the same word Lamentations uses of the city swallowed by enemies (Lam 2:5). The worthless vessel (keli ein chefetz, a vessel in which there is no pleasure/desire) is the pottery that has lost its function. Israel's purpose was to be a vessel of divine blessing among nations; now they are scattered among them as a broken, unwanted pot.
9: ki hem alu Ashur pere boded lo lahem: 'For they have gone up to Assyria — Ephraim is like a wild donkey going off alone; they have hired lovers.' The pere (wild donkey) is the symbol of wild, untameable self-direction — cf. Gen 16:12 (Ishmael will be like a wild donkey among men). The wild donkey that wanders alone contrasts with the domesticated animal that serves in community. Israel has gone alone (boded) to seek foreign alliances — 'hired lovers' (mehanim ahavim) — the covenant prostitution metaphor: paying foreign powers for the 'love' (alliance, protection) that YHWH alone legitimately provides.
10: gam ki yiskor bagovim attah aqabbetzem: 'Even though they hire allies among the nations, I will now gather them in.' The divine gathering (qabbetz) that Israel seeks by hiring allies will come — but as judgment, not rescue. Vayachelu me'at mas'et melekh sarim: 'They will begin to diminish under the burden of king and princes' — the very foreign powers they paid for protection will burden them instead. The irony of covenant judgment: the thing Israel pursued for salvation becomes the instrument of its diminishment.
11: ki hirbah Efraim mizbechot lachto: 'Since Ephraim has multiplied altars for sinning, those altars serve only to produce more sin.' The multiplication of cult sites (mizbeachot) that was supposed to multiply worship instead multiplies sin. This is the idolatry spiral: each additional altar is an additional occasion for transgression rather than an additional means of approaching YHWH. The means designed for righteousness become, when perverted, engines of corruption — the altar built for sin produces nothing but more sin.
12: ichtov lo ribevo torati kemozar nechshavu: 'Though I wrote for him the great things of my law, they were treated as something foreign.' The written Torah — YHWH has katav (written) the ten thousand things (ribevo) of his instruction — is treated as zar (strange, foreign). The Torah given specifically to Israel is experienced by them as alien, belonging to another people. This is the covenant paradox: the most intimate divine self-disclosure (written law, personal instruction) is received as if it were someone else's document. The book (ketav) that should be the dearest possession is treated as an imposition.
13: zivchei chabai yizbechu vasar vayochelu YHWH lo ratzam: 'They offer their sacrificial meat and eat it, but the LORD takes no pleasure in them.' The word chabai (my love gifts? my sacrificial gifts) is a hapax — possibly referring to 'love offerings' or sacrifices with a relational connotation. The sacrifices are performed correctly by external standards, but YHWH takes no pleasure (lo ratzam) — the same formula used of Israel's rejected worship in Amos 5:21-22. 'Now he will remember their iniquity and punish their sins; they will return to Egypt' — returning to Egypt (Mitzraim yashuvun) is the ultimate covenant reversal: the exodus undone, back to the starting point of slavery.
14: vayishkach Yisrael et oseihu vayiven heichalot: 'For Israel has forgotten his Maker and built temples.' Oseihu (his Maker) — the creator-creature relationship is the bedrock of covenant; to forget the Maker is to lose the interpretive framework for everything. They substitute heichalot (palaces/great temples) for the relationship with the One who made them. Judah is also indicted: 'multiplied fortified cities' (hirbah arim beturot) — trust in military infrastructure rather than in YHWH. 'But I will send fire upon his cities; it will devour his strongholds' — the fire of judgment consumes the very fortifications they built as alternatives to covenant trust.
